The **Gold Coast**, also known by its initials, **GC**, is a coastal city and region in the state of [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), Australia,[[4]](#cite_note-4) located approximately 66 kilometres (41 mi) south-southeast of the centre of the [state capital](/wiki/List_of_Australian_capital_cities "List of Australian capital cities"), [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"). It is on the central eastern coast of Australia facing the Pacific Ocean. It is Queensland's [second-largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Queensland_by_population "List of cities in Queensland by population") after Brisbane, as well as Australia's [sixth-largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Australia_by_population "List of cities in Australia by population") and the most populous non-capital city.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The city's [central business district](/wiki/Gold_Coast_central_business_district "Gold Coast central business district") is located roughly in the centre of the Gold Coast in the suburb of [Southport](/wiki/Southport,_Queensland "Southport, Queensland").[[6]](#cite_note-6) The urban area of the Gold Coast is concentrated along the coast, sprawling almost 60 kilometres, joining up with the Greater Brisbane metropolitan region to the north and to the state border with [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") to the south.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Nicknames of the city include the ‘Glitter Strip’ and the ‘Goldy’. The [demonym](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") of a Gold Coast resident is Gold Coaster.

The area that became the Gold Coast was originally inhabited by the indigenous [Yugambeh people](/wiki/Yugambeh_people "Yugambeh people"). The city grew from a collection of small townships, the earliest being [Nerang](/wiki/Nerang,_Queensland "Nerang, Queensland") in 1865. From the 1920s onwards, tourism led to significant economic growth in the region, and by 1959 the Gold Coast was declared a city, with its [first high-rise](/wiki/Kinkabool "Kinkabool") being built in 1960. The Gold Coast boomed from the 1980s onwards with extensive [skyscraper construction](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_on_the_Gold_Coast "List of tallest buildings on the Gold Coast"). This era was defined by the city's 'white-shoe brigade' developers, [neon lights](/wiki/Neon_lighting "Neon lighting"), and [organised crime](/wiki/Organised_crime_in_Australia "Organised crime in Australia"), particularly the [yakuza](/wiki/Yakuza "Yakuza") and the [Russian mafia](/wiki/Russian_mafia "Russian mafia").[[8]](#cite_note-8) The late 20th century saw the city's tourism diversify with theme park openings, and in the early 21st century became an international destination for film production.

The Gold Coast has a diverse economy with strengths in health, tourism, arts and culture, and construction, with a GDP of [AU$](/wiki/Australian_dollar "Australian dollar")49.3 billion as of 2024.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The city ranks highly as one of the country's cultural and creative hotspots,[[10]](#cite_note-10) alongside [content creators](/wiki/Creator_economy "Creator economy"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) a growing [video games industry](/wiki/Video_games_in_Australia "Video games in Australia"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) and leads Australia in [startups](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company") per capita.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
The Gold Coast is central to the nation's entertainment industry with a [major film and television](/wiki/List_of_films_shot_on_the_Gold_Coast "List of films shot on the Gold Coast") production industry, leading to the city's metonym of [Goldywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_Australia "Cinema of Australia").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) The Gold Coast is also host of the [AACTA Awards](/wiki/AACTA_Awards "AACTA Awards"), the [Asia Pacific Screen Awards](/wiki/Asia_Pacific_Screen_Awards "Asia Pacific Screen Awards"), and the [Gold Coast Film Festival](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Film_Festival "Gold Coast Film Festival").

The Gold Coast is a major [tourist destination](/wiki/Tourist_destination "Tourist destination") with a sunny, [subtropical climate](/wiki/Subtropical_climate "Subtropical climate") and has become widely known for its [surfing](/wiki/Surfing "Surfing") [beaches](/wiki/Beaches "Beaches") (such as [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise,_Queensland "Surfers Paradise, Queensland")), high-rise buildings, [theme parks](/wiki/Theme_parks "Theme parks"), [nightlife](/wiki/Nightlife "Nightlife"), and [rainforest](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest") [hinterland](/wiki/Gold_Coast_hinterland "Gold Coast hinterland").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Gold Coast, Queensland](/wiki/History_of_the_Gold_Coast,_Queensland "History of the Gold Coast, Queensland")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Gold Coast, Queensland](/wiki/Timeline_of_Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Timeline of Gold Coast, Queensland").

The Gold Coast is the ancestral home of a number of Indigenous clans of the Yugambeh people,[[16]](#cite_note-16) including the [Kombumerri](/wiki/Kombumerri_clan "Kombumerri clan"),[[17]](#cite_note-17) [Mununjali](/wiki/Mununjali_clan "Mununjali clan"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) and Wangerriburra clans. Europeans arrived in 1823 when explorer [John Oxley](/wiki/John_Oxley "John Oxley") explored the Tweed River.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The hinterland's timber supply attracted people to the area in the mid-19th century.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Map_of_the_Gold_Coast_from_Coomera_Island_to_Nobby_Beach%2C_1887.jpg/250px-Map_of_the_Gold_Coast_from_Coomera_Island_to_Nobby_Beach%2C_1887.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Gold_Coast_from_Coomera_Island_to_Nobby_Beach,_1887.jpg)

Map of the Gold Coast, 1887

A number of small townships developed along the coast and in the hinterland. The western suburb of [Nerang](/wiki/Nerang "Nerang") was surveyed and established as a base for the industry and by 1870 a town reserve had been set aside.[[21]](#cite_note-21) By 1873, the town reserve of [Burleigh Heads](/wiki/Burleigh_Heads "Burleigh Heads") had also been surveyed and successful land sales had taken place.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In 1875, the small settlement opposite the boat passage at the head of the [Nerang River](/wiki/Nerang_River "Nerang River"), known as Nerang Heads or Nerang Creek Heads, was surveyed and renamed [Southport](/wiki/Southport,_Queensland "Southport, Queensland"), with the first land sales scheduled to take place in Beenleigh.[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Southport](/wiki/Southport,_Queensland "Southport, Queensland") quickly grew a reputation as a secluded holiday destination for wealthy [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") residents.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/StateLibQld_1_193667_Southport_Esplanade_looking_from_the_pier%2C_ca.1928.jpg/250px-StateLibQld_1_193667_Southport_Esplanade_looking_from_the_pier%2C_ca.1928.jpg)](/wiki/File:StateLibQld_1_193667_Southport_Esplanade_looking_from_the_pier,_ca.1928.jpg)

Southport Esplanade in 1928

Post-World War One Era saw the rise of the "seaside shack". The seaside shack provided the opportunity for the coastal "getaway" with modest investment.  From 1914 to 1946, they popped up all along the South Coast. Seaside shacks were exceedingly cheap and were an early use of the concept of recycling. Many were built of disused or second grade timber, all kinds of materials were used for the holiday seaside shack – including fibro cement, metal containers, and left-over farm sheds; even disused trams were sold off as seaside shacks.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/StateLibQld_1_113720_Surfers_Paradise%2C_Gold_Coast%2C_ca._1951.jpg/250px-StateLibQld_1_113720_Surfers_Paradise%2C_Gold_Coast%2C_ca._1951.jpg)](/wiki/File:StateLibQld_1_113720_Surfers_Paradise,_Gold_Coast,_ca._1951.jpg)

Surfers Paradise, 1951

After the establishment of the [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise "Surfers Paradise") Hotel in the late 1920s, the Gold Coast region grew significantly.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) The Gold Coast was originally known as the South Coast (because it was south of Brisbane). However, over-inflated prices for real estate and other goods and services led to the nickname of "Gold Coast" from 1950.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) South Coast locals initially considered the name "Gold Coast" derogatory.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) However, soon the "Gold Coast" simply became a convenient way to refer to the holiday strip from Southport to [Coolangatta](/wiki/Coolangatta "Coolangatta").[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) The Town of South Coast was formed through the amalgamation of [Town of Coolangatta](/wiki/Town_of_Coolangatta "Town of Coolangatta") and [Town of Southport](/wiki/Town_of_Southport "Town of Southport") along with the coastal areas (such as Burleigh Heads) from the [Shire of Nerang](/wiki/Shire_of_Nerang "Shire of Nerang") on 17 June 1949,[[40]](#cite_note-40) with the effect of having the present-day Gold Coast coastal strip as a single local government area. As the tourism industry grew into the 1950s, local businesses began to adopt the term *Gold Coast* in their names, and on 23 October 1958 the [Town of South Coast](/wiki/Town_of_South_Coast "Town of South Coast") was renamed [Town of Gold Coast](/wiki/Town_of_Gold_Coast "Town of Gold Coast").[[41]](#cite_note-41) The area was proclaimed a city, less than one year later on 16 May 1959.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Cars_on_Gold_Coast_Highway%2C_Surfers_Paradise.jpg/250px-Cars_on_Gold_Coast_Highway%2C_Surfers_Paradise.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cars_on_Gold_Coast_Highway,_Surfers_Paradise.jpg)

Cars on the [Gold Coast Highway](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Highway "Gold Coast Highway"), in 1961

The area boomed in the 1980s as a leading tourist destination. In 1994, the [City of Gold Coast](/wiki/City_of_Gold_Coast "City of Gold Coast") [local government area](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia "Local government in Australia") was expanded to include the [Shire of Albert](/wiki/Shire_of_Albert "Shire of Albert").[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In 2007, the Gold Coast overtook the population of [Newcastle, New South Wales](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales"), to become the sixth largest city in Australia and the largest non-capital city.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The Gold Coast hosted the [2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games").[[45]](#cite_note-CWHost-45)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Demographics")]

In the [2016 census](/wiki/2016_Australian_census "2016 Australian census"), the urban area of the Gold Coast had a population of 540,559 people.[[46]](#cite_note-Census2016-46) According to the [2016 census](/wiki/2016_Australian_census "2016 Australian census"), the population of the Gold Coast including rural areas was 569,997. The median age was 39 years old, 1 year older than the nationwide median. The male-to-female ratio was 48.6-to-51.4.[[47]](#cite_note-abs2016pop-47) The most commonly nominated ancestries were English (29.3%), Australian (22.5%), Irish (8.2%), Scottish (7.5%), and German (3.6%). 64% of people were born in Australia, while the other most common countries of birth were New Zealand (7.9%), England (5.2%), China and South Africa (1.2% each), and Japan (0.7%). Indigenous Australians accounted for 1.7% of the population.[[47]](#cite_note-abs2016pop-47) The most commonly spoken languages other than English were Mandarin (1.6%), Japanese (1.0%), Korean and Spanish (0.6% each), and Cantonese (0.5%).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

According to the Australian Official Census in 2021, the most common religious affiliations in Gold Coast reported were none (43.4%), Catholic (18.2%), Anglican (11.9%), Uniting Church (2.9%), Non-denominational Christian (3.1%), Presbyterian and Reformed (1.9%), Buddhism (1.4%), Pentecostal (1.3%), Baptist (1.1%), Hinduism (1.1%) and Islam (1.0%).[[48]](#cite_note-:1-48)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Purling_Brook_Falls_lookout_Gold_Coast.jpg/250px-Purling_Brook_Falls_lookout_Gold_Coast.jpg)](/wiki/File:Purling_Brook_Falls_lookout_Gold_Coast.jpg)

View from the lookout at [Purling Brook Falls](/wiki/Purling_Brook_Falls "Purling Brook Falls") in the [Gold Coast hinterland](/wiki/Gold_Coast_hinterland "Gold Coast hinterland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Goldcoast_Queensland_Australia_aerial_view.jpg/250px-Goldcoast_Queensland_Australia_aerial_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goldcoast_Queensland_Australia_aerial_view.jpg)

Aerial view of Gold Coast suburbs: [Mermaid Waters](/wiki/Mermaid_Waters "Mermaid Waters") (left) and [Broadbeach Waters](/wiki/Broadbeach_Waters,_Queensland "Broadbeach Waters, Queensland") (right). The image depicts the man-made [canals](/wiki/Canals "Canals") of the city, built to accommodate housing development.

The Gold Coast is approximately half covered by forests of various types. This includes small patches of near-pristine ancient rainforest, mangrove-covered islands, and patches of coastal heathlands and farmland with areas of uncleared eucalyptus forest. Of the plantation pine forests that were planted in the 1950s and 1960s, when commercial forest planting for tax minimisation was encouraged by the Commonwealth government, tiny remnants remain.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Most of the Gold Coast area was covered by forest prior to European human settlement and was exposed to extensive land clearing in the 19th century.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Gold Coast City lies in the southeast corner of [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), to the south of [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), the state capital. The [Albert River](/wiki/Albert_River,_Queensland "Albert River, Queensland") separates the Gold Coast from [Logan City](/wiki/Logan_City,_Queensland "Logan City, Queensland"), a local government area south of the [City of Brisbane](/wiki/City_of_Brisbane "City of Brisbane").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Gold Coast City stretches from the Albert River, [Logan River](/wiki/Logan_River "Logan River"), and [Southern Moreton Bay](/wiki/Southern_Moreton_Bay_Islands_(Redland_City) "Southern Moreton Bay Islands (Redland City)") to the border with [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") (NSW) approximately 56 km (35 mi) south, and extends from the coast west to the foothills of the [Great Dividing Range](/wiki/Great_Dividing_Range "Great Dividing Range") in [World Heritage](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") listed [Lamington National Park](/wiki/Lamington_National_Park "Lamington National Park").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Goldcoast_skyline_seen_from_Coolangatta.jpg/250px-Goldcoast_skyline_seen_from_Coolangatta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goldcoast_skyline_seen_from_Coolangatta.jpg)

Gold Coast skyline seen from Coolangatta

The southernmost town of Gold Coast City, [Coolangatta](/wiki/Coolangatta,_Queensland "Coolangatta, Queensland"), includes [Point Danger](/wiki/Point_Danger_(Tweed_Heads) "Point Danger (Tweed Heads)") and its [lighthouse](/wiki/Lighthouse "Lighthouse"). Coolangatta is a twin city with [Tweed Heads](/wiki/Tweed_Heads "Tweed Heads") located directly across the NSW border. At [28°10′00″S 153°33′00″E﻿ / ﻿28.1667°S 153.55°E﻿ / -28.1667; 153.55](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&params=28.1667_S_153.55_E_region:AU-QLD), this is the most easterly point on the Queensland mainland (Point Lookout on the offshore island of [North Stradbroke](/wiki/North_Stradbroke_Island "North Stradbroke Island") is slightly further east). From Coolangatta, approximately forty kilometres of holiday resorts and surfing beaches stretch north to the suburb of Main Beach, and then further on Stradbroke Island. The suburbs of [Southport](/wiki/Southport,_Queensland "Southport, Queensland") and [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise "Surfers Paradise") form the Gold Coast's commercial centre. The major river in the area is the [Nerang River](/wiki/Nerang_River "Nerang River"). Much of the land between the coastal strip and the hinterland were once [wetlands](/wiki/Wetlands "Wetlands") drained by this river, but the swamps have been converted into man-made waterways (over 260 kilometres (160 mi) in length[[51]](#cite_note-51) or over 9 times the length of the canals of [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"), Italy) and artificial islands covered in upmarket homes. The heavily developed coastal strip sits on a narrow barrier [sandbar](/wiki/Sandbar "Sandbar") between these waterways and the sea.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

To the west, the city borders a part of the [Great Dividing Range](/wiki/Great_Dividing_Range "Great Dividing Range") commonly referred to as the [Gold Coast hinterland](/wiki/Gold_Coast_hinterland "Gold Coast hinterland"). A 206 km2 (80 sq mi) section of the mountain range is protected by [Lamington National Park](/wiki/Lamington_National_Park "Lamington National Park") and has been listed as a [World Heritage](/wiki/World_Heritage "World Heritage") area in recognition of its "outstanding geological features displayed around shield volcanic craters and the high number of rare and threatened rainforest species".[[52]](#cite_note-UNESCO-52) The area attracts [bushwalkers](/wiki/Bushwalking "Bushwalking") and day-trippers. Important rainforest pollinating and seed-dispersing Black flying foxes (Pteropus alecto) are found in the area and may be heard foraging at night.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Waterways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Waterways")]

Waterfront canal living is a feature of the Gold Coast. Most canal frontage homes have pontoons. The [Gold Coast Seaway](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Seaway "Gold Coast Seaway"), between [The Spit](/wiki/Southport_Spit,_Queensland "Southport Spit, Queensland") and [South Stradbroke Island](/wiki/South_Stradbroke_Island "South Stradbroke Island"), allows vessels direct access to the Pacific Ocean from [The Broadwater](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Broadwater "Gold Coast Broadwater") and many of the city's canal estates. Breakwaters on either side of the Seaway prevent [longshore drift](/wiki/Longshore_drift "Longshore drift") and the bar from silting up. A sand pumping operation on the Spit pipes sand under the Seaway to continue this natural process.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Residential canals were first built in the Gold Coast in the 1950s and construction continues. Most canals are extensions to the Nerang River, but there are more to the south along [Tallebudgera Creek](/wiki/Tallebudgera_Creek "Tallebudgera Creek") and [Currumbin Creek](/wiki/Currumbin_Creek "Currumbin Creek") and to the north along the [Gold Coast Broadwater](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Broadwater "Gold Coast Broadwater"), [South Stradbroke Island](/wiki/South_Stradbroke_Island "South Stradbroke Island"), [Coomera River](/wiki/Coomera_River "Coomera River") and southern Moreton Bay. Early canals included Florida Gardens and [Isle of Capri](/wiki/Isle_of_Capri,_Queensland "Isle of Capri, Queensland") which were under construction at the time of a 1954 flood.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Recently constructed canals include Harbour Quays and Riverlinks completed in 2007. There are over 890 kilometres (550 mi) of constructed residential waterfront land within the city that is home to over 80,000 residents.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The city consists of 70 kilometres (43 mi) of coastline, with some of the most popular [surf](/wiki/Surfing "Surfing") breaks in Australia and the world, including [South Stradbroke Island](/wiki/South_Stradbroke_Island "South Stradbroke Island"), [The Spit](/wiki/Southport_Spit,_Queensland "Southport Spit, Queensland"), [Main Beach](/wiki/Main_Beach,_Queensland "Main Beach, Queensland"), [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise,_Queensland "Surfers Paradise, Queensland"), [Broadbeach](/wiki/Broadbeach,_Queensland "Broadbeach, Queensland"), Mermaid Beach, Nobby's Beach, [Miami](/wiki/Miami,_Queensland "Miami, Queensland"), North Burleigh Beach, Burleigh Beach, [Burleigh Heads](/wiki/Burleigh_Heads,_Queensland "Burleigh Heads, Queensland"), Tallebudgera Beach, [Palm Beach](/wiki/Palm_Beach,_Queensland "Palm Beach, Queensland"), South Palm Beach, Currumbin Beach, Tugun, [Bilinga](/wiki/Bilinga,_Queensland "Bilinga, Queensland"), North Kirra Beach [Kirra](/wiki/Kirra,_Queensland "Kirra, Queensland"), [Coolangatta](/wiki/Coolangatta,_Queensland "Coolangatta, Queensland"), [Greenmount](/wiki/Greenmount_Beach "Greenmount Beach"), Rainbow Bay, [Snapper Rocks](/wiki/Snapper_Rocks "Snapper Rocks") and Froggies Beach. There is almost 42 km of unbroken beachfront. [Duranbah Beach](/wiki/Duranbah_Beach "Duranbah Beach") is one of the world's best known [surfing](/wiki/Surfing "Surfing") beaches and is often thought of as being part of Gold Coast City, but is actually just across the [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") state border in the [Tweed Shire](/wiki/Tweed_Shire "Tweed Shire").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There are also beaches along many of the Gold Coast's 860 km (530 mi) of navigable tidal waterways. Popular inland beaches include [Southport](/wiki/Southport,_Queensland "Southport, Queensland"), Budds Beach, Marine Stadium, [Currumbin Alley](/wiki/Currumbin_Alley "Currumbin Alley"), Tallebudgera Estuary, Jacobs Well, Jabiru Island, Paradise Point, Harley Park Labrador, Santa Barbara, Boykambil and Evandale Lake.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

**Beach safety and management**

The Gold Coast has Australia's largest[[56]](#cite_note-56) professional [surf lifesaving](/wiki/Surf_lifesaving "Surf lifesaving") service to protect people on the beaches and to promote surf safety throughout the community. The [Queensland Department of Primary Industries](/wiki/Queensland_Department_of_Primary_Industries "Queensland Department of Primary Industries") carries out the Queensland Shark Control Program (SCP) to protect swimmers from sharks.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Sharks are caught by using nets and baited drumlines off the major swimming beaches. Even with the SCP, sharks do range within sight of the [patrolled beaches](/wiki/Beach_patrol "Beach patrol"). [Lifeguards](/wiki/Lifeguard "Lifeguard") will clear swimmers from the water if it is considered that there is a safety risk.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Gold Coast beaches have experienced periods of severe [beach erosion](/wiki/Coastal_erosion "Coastal erosion"). In 1967, a series of 11 cyclones removed most of the sand from Gold Coast beaches. The [Government of Queensland](/wiki/Government_of_Queensland "Government of Queensland") engaged engineers from [Delft University](/wiki/Delft_University "Delft University") in the Netherlands to advise what to do about the beach erosion. The Delft Report[[58]](#cite_note-58) was published in 1971 and outlined a series of works for Gold Coast Beaches including [Gold Coast Seaway](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Seaway "Gold Coast Seaway"),[[59]](#cite_note-59) works at [Narrow Neck](/wiki/Narrow_Neck,_Queensland "Narrow Neck, Queensland") that resulted in the Northern Gold Coast Beach Protection Strategy[[60]](#cite_note-60) and works at the [Tweed River](/wiki/Tweed_River_(New_South_Wales) "Tweed River (New South Wales)") that became the [Tweed River](/wiki/Tweed_River_(New_South_Wales) "Tweed River (New South Wales)") Entrance Sand Bypassing Project.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

By 2005 most of the recommendations of the 1971 Delft Report had been implemented. [City of Gold Coast](/wiki/City_of_Gold_Coast "City of Gold Coast") commenced implementation of the [Palm Beach](/wiki/Palm_Beach,_Queensland "Palm Beach, Queensland") Protection Strategy[[62]](#cite_note-62) but ran into considerable opposition from the community participating in a NO REEF protest campaign.[[63]](#cite_note-63) The [City of Gold Coast Council](/wiki/City_of_Gold_Coast_Council "City of Gold Coast Council") then committed to completing a review of beach management practices to update the Delft Report. The [Gold Coast Shoreline Management Plan](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Shoreline_Management_Plan "Gold Coast Shoreline Management Plan")[[64]](#cite_note-64) will be delivered by organisations including the [Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/Environmental_Protection_Agency_(Queensland) "Environmental Protection Agency (Queensland)"), [City of Gold Coast](/wiki/City_of_Gold_Coast "City of Gold Coast") and the Griffith Centre for Coastal Management. Gold Coast City is also investing into the quality and capacity of the [Gold Coast Oceanway](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Oceanway "Gold Coast Oceanway") that provides [sustainable transport](/wiki/Sustainable_transport "Sustainable transport") along Gold Coast beaches.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Climate")]

The Gold Coast experiences a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *[Cfa](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate")*), with mild to warm winters and hot, humid summers. The city experiences substantial summer precipitation mostly concentrated in thunderstorms and heavy showers with rain events occasionally lasting up to a few weeks at a time,[[66]](#cite_note-66) while winter is pleasantly mild to warm with little rain. In fact, it is for this pleasant winter weather that both the city and the [Sunshine Coast](/wiki/Sunshine_Coast,_Queensland "Sunshine Coast, Queensland")—the coastal region north of Brisbane—are internationally renowned.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Extreme temperatures recorded at [Gold Coast Seaway](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Seaway "Gold Coast Seaway") have ranged from 2.5 °C (36 °F) on 19 July 2007 to 40.5 °C (105 °F) on 22 February 2005, although the city rarely experiences temperatures above 35 °C (95 °F) in summer or below 5 °C (41 °F) in winter.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The average temperature of the sea at [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise "Surfers Paradise") ranges from 21.5 °C (70.7 °F) in July and August to 27.1 °C (80.8 °F) in February.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Government")]

Main articles: [City of Gold Coast](/wiki/City_of_Gold_Coast "City of Gold Coast") and [Government of Queensland](/wiki/Government_of_Queensland "Government of Queensland")

Administratively, the Gold Coast is a local government area called the [City of Gold Coast](/wiki/City_of_Gold_Coast "City of Gold Coast"). The City of Gold Coast Council has 14 elected councillors, each representing a division of the city. Businessman Tom Tate is the current [Mayor of the Gold Coast](/wiki/Mayor_of_the_Gold_Coast "Mayor of the Gold Coast"), first elected in 2012. Former mayors include [Ron Clake](/wiki/Ron_Clarke "Ron Clarke"), Gary Baildon, [Lex Bell](/wiki/Lex_Bell "Lex Bell"), [Ray Stevens](/wiki/Ray_Stevens_(politician) "Ray Stevens (politician)"), Ern Harley and [Sir Bruce Small](/wiki/Bruce_Small "Bruce Small"), who was responsible for the development of many of the canal estates that are now home to thousands of Gold Coast residents.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At the state level, the Gold Coast area is represented by eleven members in the [Legislative Assembly of Queensland](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Queensland "Legislative Assembly of Queensland"). The seats they hold are: [Bonney](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Bonney "Electoral district of Bonney"), [Broadwater](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Broadwater "Electoral district of Broadwater"), [Burleigh](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Burleigh "Electoral district of Burleigh"), [Coomera](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Coomera "Electoral district of Coomera"), [Currumbin](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Currumbin "Electoral district of Currumbin"), [Gaven](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Gaven "Electoral district of Gaven"), [Mermaid Beach](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Mermaid_Beach "Electoral district of Mermaid Beach"), [Mudgeeraba](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Mudgeeraba "Electoral district of Mudgeeraba"), [Southport](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Southport "Electoral district of Southport"), [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Surfers_Paradise "Electoral district of Surfers Paradise") and [Theodore](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Theodore "Electoral district of Theodore"). Federally, the Gold Coast area is split between five divisions in the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Australian_House_of_Representatives "Australian House of Representatives"): [Fadden](/wiki/Division_of_Fadden "Division of Fadden") (northern), [Moncrieff](/wiki/Division_of_Moncrieff "Division of Moncrieff") (central) and [McPherson](/wiki/Division_of_McPherson "Division of McPherson") (southern) are located entirely within the Gold Coast, while [Forde](/wiki/Division_of_Forde "Division of Forde") (north-west) and [Wright](/wiki/Division_of_Wright "Division of Wright") (south-west) encompass parts of the Gold Coast and other areas of Southeast Queensland.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Southport_Courthouse.jpg/250px-Southport_Courthouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Southport_Courthouse.jpg)

[Southport Courthouse](/wiki/Southport_Courthouse "Southport Courthouse")

Politically, the Gold Coast has often tilted conservative.[[72]](#cite_note-72) It was a [Country Party](/wiki/National_Party_of_Australia "National Party of Australia") bastion for most of the first three decades after World War II, but increasing urbanisation has made it a [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Australia "Liberal Party of Australia") stronghold. [Labor](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party") has historically only done well around Labrador and Coolangatta. Only one Labor MP has ever represented a significant portion of the Gold Coast at the federal level since 1949; the three Gold Coast divisions have only returned Liberals since 1984. At the state level, Labor was fairly competitive in the Gold Coast for most of the early part of the 21st century. However, as part of its massive landslide in the [2012 state election](/wiki/2012_Queensland_state_election "2012 Queensland state election"), the [Liberal National Party](/wiki/Liberal_National_Party_of_Queensland "Liberal National Party of Queensland") won every seat there. The LNP repeated its sweep of the Gold Coast seats at the [2015 election](/wiki/2015_Queensland_state_election "2015 Queensland state election"), and retained all but one Gold Coast seat at the [2017 state election](/wiki/2017_Queensland_state_election "2017 Queensland state election").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Southport Courthouse](/wiki/Southport_Courthouse "Southport Courthouse") is the city's major courthouse and has jurisdiction to hear petty criminal offences and civil matters up to A$250,000. Indictable offences, criminal sentencing and civil matters above A$250,000 are heard in the higher [Supreme Court of Queensland](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Queensland "Supreme Court of Queensland") which is located in Brisbane. There is also a subsidiary Magistrates Court, located at the southern suburb of [Coolangatta](/wiki/Coolangatta,_Queensland "Coolangatta, Queensland").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2013 a brawl between members of [Outlaw motorcycle gangs](/wiki/Outlaw_motorcycle_gangs_in_Australia "Outlaw motorcycle gangs in Australia") also called "bikies" who fought each other outside a [Broadbeach](/wiki/Broadbeach "Broadbeach") restaurant caused mass fear to restaurant patrons and police.[[73]](#cite_note-73) This led to the toughest anti-bikie laws introduced in Australia known as [Vicious Lawless Association Disestablishment Act 2013](/wiki/Vicious_Lawless_Association_Disestablishment_Act_2013 "Vicious Lawless Association Disestablishment Act 2013").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Gold-Coast-Skyline-at-Night.jpg/250px-Gold-Coast-Skyline-at-Night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gold-Coast-Skyline-at-Night.jpg)

Gold Coast CBD, 2011

In fifty years, Gold Coast City has grown from a small beachside holiday destination to Australia's sixth largest city (and the country's most populous non-capital city). Situated within South East Queensland's growth corridor, the Gold Coast is one of Australia's fastest growing large cities, with a 5-year annual average population growth rate to 2015 of 1.8%, compared to 1.5% nationally.[[74]](#cite_note-ABSSUA15-74) Gross Regional Product has risen from A$9.7 billion in 2001, to A$15.6 billion in 2008, a rise of 61 percent.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Tourism remains fundamental to Gold Coast City's economy, with almost 10 million visitors a year to the area.[[76]](#cite_note-76) In the past the economy was driven by the population derived industries of construction, tourism and retail. Some diversification has taken place, with the city now having an industrial base formed of marine, education, information communication and technology, food, tourism, creative, environment and sports industries. These nine industries have been identified as the key industries by the [City of Gold Coast Council](/wiki/City_of_Gold_Coast_Council "City of Gold Coast Council") to deliver the city's economic prosperity. Gold Coast City's unemployment rate (5.6 per cent) is below the national level (5.9 per cent).[[77]](#cite_note-77) The declaration of Southport as the [Gold Coast central business district](/wiki/Gold_Coast_central_business_district "Gold Coast central business district") (CBD) and a Priority Development Area (PDA), as well as new investment into the CBD, is driving transformative change and creating new business and investment opportunities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Gold Coast Economic Development Strategy 2013–2023[[78]](#cite_note-78) outlines the framework for the city's long-term growth and prosperity. The strategy outlines actions in the following areas, Innovation, Culture, Infrastructure, Competitive business, Workforce, International.

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Palazzo_Versace_Gold_Coast%2C_Australia.jpg/250px-Palazzo_Versace_Gold_Coast%2C_Australia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palazzo_Versace_Gold_Coast,_Australia.jpg)

The [hospitality](/wiki/Hospitality "Hospitality") industry contributes significantly to the Gold Coast's economy. The [Palazzo Versace](/wiki/Palazzo_Versace_Australia "Palazzo Versace Australia") is a notable hotel on [Main Beach](/wiki/Main_Beach,_Queensland "Main Beach, Queensland").

The Gold Coast is the most popular tourist destination in Queensland.[[79]](#cite_note-79) It is Australia's 5th most visited destination by international tourists.[[80]](#cite_note-80) Around 10 million tourists visit the Gold Coast area every year consisting of 849,114 international visitors, 3,468,000 domestic overnight visitors and 5,366,000 daytrip visitors. Tourism is the region's biggest industry,[[81]](#cite_note-81) directly contributing more than $4.4 billion into the city economy every year and directly accounting for one in four jobs in the city[[82]](#cite_note-82) There are approximately 65,000 beds, 60 kilometres (37 mi) of beach, 600 kilometres (370 mi) of canal, 100,000 hectares of nature reserve, 500 restaurants, 40 golf courses and five major theme parks in the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Gold Coast Airport](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Airport "Gold Coast Airport") provides connection across Australia and internationally with airlines including [Jetstar](/wiki/Jetstar "Jetstar"), [Qantas](/wiki/Qantas "Qantas"), [Air New Zealand](/wiki/Air_New_Zealand "Air New Zealand"), [Scoot](/wiki/Scoot "Scoot"), [Virgin Australia](/wiki/Virgin_Australia "Virgin Australia") and [Airasia X](/wiki/Airasia_X "Airasia X"). [Brisbane Airport](/wiki/Brisbane_Airport "Brisbane Airport") is less than one hour from the centre of Gold Coast, and [direct trains](/wiki/Airport_railway_line,_Brisbane "Airport railway line, Brisbane") operate.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Sea_World_Entrance-1and_%284505997395%29.jpg/250px-Sea_World_Entrance-1and_%284505997395%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sea_World_Entrance-1and_(4505997395).jpg)

[Sea World](/wiki/Sea_World_(Australia) "Sea World (Australia)"), one of many theme parks on the Gold Coast

Gold Coast City has over 13,000 available guest rooms contributing over $335 million to the local economy each year. Accommodation options available range from [hostels](/wiki/Hostel "Hostel") to five star resorts and hotels. Tourist attractions include surf beaches. The big attractions are the numerous theme parks in the area. Theme parks include [Dreamworld](/wiki/Dreamworld_(Australian_theme_park) "Dreamworld (Australian theme park)"), [Sea World](/wiki/Sea_World_(Australia) "Sea World (Australia)"), [Wet'n'Wild Water World](/wiki/Wet%27n%27Wild_Water_World "Wet'n'Wild Water World"), [Warner Bros. Movie World](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Movie_World "Warner Bros. Movie World"), [WhiteWater World](/wiki/WhiteWater_World "WhiteWater World"), [Topgolf](/wiki/Topgolf "Topgolf"), [Currumbin Wildlife Sanctuary](/wiki/Currumbin_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Currumbin Wildlife Sanctuary"), [David Fleay Wildlife Park](/wiki/David_Fleay_Wildlife_Park "David Fleay Wildlife Park"), Australian Outback Spectacular, and Paradise Country.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Q1_seen_from_the_Isle_of_Capri%2C_Queensland%2C_2023%2C_02_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Q1_seen_from_the_Isle_of_Capri%2C_Queensland%2C_2023%2C_02_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Q1_seen_from_the_Isle_of_Capri,_Queensland,_2023,_02_(cropped).jpg)

[Q1](/wiki/Q1_(building) "Q1 (building)"), the [tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Australia "List of tallest buildings in Australia") in Australia and the [world's tallest residential building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_residential_buildings_in_the_world "List of tallest residential buildings in the world") upon completion in 2005 (currently the [seventeenth tallest](/wiki/List_of_tallest_residential_buildings "List of tallest residential buildings"))

Since the opening of what was then the world's highest residential tower in 2005 (it is now the 17th highest), the [Q1 building](/wiki/Q1_(building) "Q1 (building)") has been a destination for tourists and locals alike. It is the second highest public vantage point in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") after the [Eureka Tower](/wiki/Eureka_Tower "Eureka Tower") in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"). The observation deck at level 77 is the highest of its kind in [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") and offers views in all directions, from [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") to [Byron Bay](/wiki/Byron_Bay "Byron Bay"). It towers over the [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise "Surfers Paradise") skyline, with the observation deck 230 metres (750 feet) high, and the spire extending nearly another hundred metres up. In total, the Q1 is 322.5 metres (1,058 feet) high, making it the [tallest building in Australia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Australia "List of tallest buildings in Australia"). Another famous tourist attraction are the [Surfers Paradise Meter Maids](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise_Meter_Maids "Surfers Paradise Meter Maids"), instituted in 1965 to put a positive spin on new parking regulations. To avoid tickets being issued for expired parking, the Meter Maids dispense coins into the meter and leave a calling card under the windscreen wiper of the vehicle. The Maids are still a part of the Surfers Paradise culture but the scheme is now run by private enterprise.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Chinatown, Gold Coast](/wiki/Chinatown,_Gold_Coast "Chinatown, Gold Coast"), is an integral part of the revitalisation of Southport as an international CBD.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

### Film production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Film production")]

See also: [List of films shot on the Gold Coast](/wiki/List_of_films_shot_on_the_Gold_Coast "List of films shot on the Gold Coast")

The Gold Coast is the major film production hub in Queensland and has accounted for 75%[[84]](#cite_note-84) of all film production in Queensland since the 1990s, with an expenditure of around $150 million per year. The Gold Coast is the third largest film production centre in Australia, behind Sydney and Melbourne.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Grand_Entrance_-_Warner_Bros._Movie_World.jpg/250px-Grand_Entrance_-_Warner_Bros._Movie_World.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Entrance_-_Warner_Bros._Movie_World.jpg)

[Warner Bros. Movie World](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Movie_World "Warner Bros. Movie World")

It is the filming site for major motion pictures including *[Muriel's Wedding](/wiki/Muriel%27s_Wedding "Muriel's Wedding")* (1994), *[Ghost Ship](/wiki/Ghost_Ship_(2002_film) "Ghost Ship (2002 film)")* (2002), *[Scooby-Doo](/wiki/Scooby-Doo_(film) "Scooby-Doo (film)")* (2002), *[House of Wax](/wiki/House_of_Wax_(2005_film) "House of Wax (2005 film)")* (2005), *[Superman Returns](/wiki/Superman_Returns "Superman Returns")* (2006), *[Unbroken](/wiki/Unbroken_(film) "Unbroken (film)")* (2014), *[The Inbetweeners 2](/wiki/The_Inbetweeners_2 "The Inbetweeners 2")* (2014), *[San Andreas](/wiki/San_Andreas_(film) "San Andreas (film)")* (2015), *[Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Men Tell No Tales](/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean:_Dead_Men_Tell_No_Tales "Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Men Tell No Tales")* (2017), *[Kong: Skull Island](/wiki/Kong:_Skull_Island "Kong: Skull Island")* (2017), *[Thor: Ragnarok](/wiki/Thor:_Ragnarok "Thor: Ragnarok")* (2017), *[Pacific Rim: Uprising](/wiki/Pacific_Rim:_Uprising "Pacific Rim: Uprising")* (2018), *[Aquaman](/wiki/Aquaman_(film) "Aquaman (film)")* (2018), *[Dora the Explorer](/wiki/Dora_the_Explorer_(film) "Dora the Explorer (film)")* (2019)[[85]](#cite_note-85) and *[Godzilla vs. Kong](/wiki/Godzilla_vs._Kong "Godzilla vs. Kong")* (2021).[[86]](#cite_note-86)

[Village Roadshow Studios](/wiki/Village_Roadshow_Studios "Village Roadshow Studios") are adjacent to the Warner Bros Movie World Theme Park at [Oxenford](/wiki/Oxenford,_Queensland "Oxenford, Queensland"). The Studios consists of eight sound stages, production offices, editing rooms, wardrobe, construction workshops, water tanks and commissary. These sound stages vary in size and have an overall floor area of 10,844 sq metres, making Warner Roadshow Studio one of the largest studio lots in the Southern Hemisphere. The Queensland Government actively supports the film and television production industry in Queensland and provides both non-financial and financial assistance through the Pacific Film and Television Commission.[[87]](#cite_note-87) The Park used to be another film studio.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Home_of_the_Arts%2C_Surfers_Paradise%2C_Gold_Coast%2C_2023%2C_04.jpg/250px-Home_of_the_Arts%2C_Surfers_Paradise%2C_Gold_Coast%2C_2023%2C_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Home_of_the_Arts,_Surfers_Paradise,_Gold_Coast,_2023,_04.jpg)

[Home of the Arts](/wiki/Home_of_the_Arts,_Gold_Coast "Home of the Arts, Gold Coast")

The Gold Coast's culture has been affected by rapid development and traditional marketing programs orbiting around 'sun, sand, surf and sex.'[[88]](#cite_note-88)

Despite rapid socio-economic changes and a tourist-centred image, there is evidence of local resident-driven culture (such as [surf gangs](/wiki/Surf_culture#Surf_gangs "Surf culture")) in geographical pockets and a broader 'Gold Coaster' identity drawn from [globalised](/wiki/Globalization#Cultural_globalization "Globalization") resort and real estate marketing material.[[89]](#cite_note-89) The Gold Coast hosts cultural activities that attract tourists and residents alike.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Music")]

Music groups in this region include the [Northern Rivers Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Northern_Rivers_Symphony_Orchestra "Northern Rivers Symphony Orchestra") and [Operator Please](/wiki/Operator_Please "Operator Please"). Musicians [Cody Simpson](/wiki/Cody_Simpson "Cody Simpson") and [Ricki-Lee Coulter](/wiki/Ricki-Lee_Coulter "Ricki-Lee Coulter") are from the Gold Coast. Music events include [Big Day Out](/wiki/Big_Day_Out "Big Day Out"), [Good Vibrations Festival](/w/index.php?title=Good_Vibrations_Festival&action=edit&redlink=1 "Good Vibrations Festival (page does not exist)"), Summafieldayze, the Blues on Broadbeach Festival and [V Festival](/wiki/V_Festival_(Australia) "V Festival (Australia)") (2007–2009).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Arts")]

[Home of the Arts](/wiki/Home_of_the_Arts,_Gold_Coast "Home of the Arts, Gold Coast") (HOTA) is the Gold Coast's premier cultural facility for visual and performing arts with a performance theatre, two cinemas and an underground venue. The theatre has hosted performance by the [Imperial Russian Ballet](/wiki/Imperial_Russian_Ballet "Imperial Russian Ballet"), [The Australian Ballet](/wiki/The_Australian_Ballet "The Australian Ballet") and the [Queensland Ballet](/wiki/Queensland_Ballet "Queensland Ballet"). Musicals, plays and a variety of performances are regularly scheduled. The city is also home to the [Gold Coast City Art Gallery](/wiki/Gold_Coast_City_Art_Gallery "Gold Coast City Art Gallery"). Film festivals and the Comedy Club host international artists.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] A redeveloped Gold Coast cultural precinct opened before the city hosted the [2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games").[[90]](#cite_note-90)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sport")]

Main article: [Sports on the Gold Coast, Queensland](/wiki/Sports_on_the_Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Sports on the Gold Coast, Queensland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Sunny_Carrara_Stadium.jpg/250px-Sunny_Carrara_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sunny_Carrara_Stadium.jpg)

[People First Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Stadium "Carrara Stadium"), home of the [Gold Coast Suns](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Suns "Gold Coast Suns") in the [Australian Football League](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League")[[91]](#cite_note-91)

The two most popular sports on the Gold Coast are [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football") and [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"),[[92]](#cite_note-92) of which the city is represented by professional teams in two most popular national competitions:

| [Gold Coast Suns](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Suns "Gold Coast Suns") | [Australian Football League](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League") | [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football") | 2011–present |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Gold_Coast_Convention_and_Exhibition_Centre_main_entrance.jpg/250px-Gold_Coast_Convention_and_Exhibition_Centre_main_entrance.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gold_Coast_Convention_and_Exhibition_Centre_main_entrance.jpg)

[Gold Coast Convention & Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Convention_%26_Exhibition_Centre "Gold Coast Convention & Exhibition Centre")

[Burleigh Bears](/wiki/Burleigh_Bears "Burleigh Bears") [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") football club play in the [Queensland Cup](/wiki/Queensland_Cup "Queensland Cup") and have won four premierships (in 1999, 2004, 2016 and 2019).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Recreational activities on the Gold Coast include surfing, fishing, cycling, boating and golf. The Gold Coast area has numerous [golf links](/wiki/Golf_links "Golf links"), including [Hope Island](/wiki/Hope_Island,_Queensland "Hope Island, Queensland"), [Sanctuary Cove](/wiki/Sanctuary_Cove "Sanctuary Cove") and The Glades.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Sporting facilities include the [Carrara Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Stadium "Carrara Stadium"), Carrara Indoor Sport Centre, [Nerang Velodrome](/wiki/Nerang_Velodrome "Nerang Velodrome") and the Sports Super Centre. Newer facilities such as the [Gold coast sports and Leisure center](http://Gold%20Coast%20Sports%20and%20Leisure%20Centre)[*[dead link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link_rot "Wikipedia:Link rot")*], [Runaway Bay Indoor Sports Stadium](https://www.goldcoast.qld.gov.au/Services/Venues-facilities/Sports-venues/Runaway-Bay-Indoor-Stadium), [Pimpama Sports Hub](https://www.goldcoast.qld.gov.au/Services/Venues-facilities/Sports-venues/Pimpama-Sports-Hub), and the [Gold Coast Sports Precinct](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Sports_Precinct "Gold Coast Sports Precinct") have been built to accommodate the growing population.

Former [World Wrestling Entertainment](/wiki/WWE "WWE") performer [Nathan Jones](/wiki/Nathan_Jones_(wrestler) "Nathan Jones (wrestler)") comes from the Gold Coast, as do Olympic gold medal-winning swimmer [Grant Hackett](/wiki/Grant_Hackett "Grant Hackett"), [2011 US Open](/wiki/2011_US_Open_(tennis) "2011 US Open (tennis)") tennis champion [Samantha Stosur](/wiki/Samantha_Stosur "Samantha Stosur") and [Sally Pearson](/wiki/Sally_Pearson "Sally Pearson") (who received the keys to the city).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Gold Coast has garnered a reputation as a "sporting graveyard", as many of the professional clubs that have represented the Gold Coast in national leagues since the 1980s experience generally poor on-field performances, consistently struggle to support themselves financially, and have generally folded within a decade of being founded; as of 2019 no Gold Coast-based team has won a premiership in a national professional club competition.[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95) However, The Gold Coast Suns entered their first [AFL Finals series](/wiki/AFL_finals_series "AFL finals series") in 2025 but lost in the semi finals against cross town rivals [Brisbane Lions](/wiki/Brisbane_Lions "Brisbane Lions") at [The Gabba](/wiki/The_Gabba "The Gabba").[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)

### Commonwealth Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Commonwealth Games")]

Main article: [2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/2018_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony.jpeg/250px-2018_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony.jpeg)](/wiki/File:2018_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony.jpeg)

[Opening ceremony](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony "2018 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony") of the 2018 Commonwealth Games at the [Carrara Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Stadium "Carrara Stadium")

The Gold Coast hosted the [2018 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games"), an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") for members of the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), held between 4 and 15 April 2018. It was the fifth time Australia had hosted the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") and the first time a major multi-sport event achieved [gender equality](/wiki/Gender_equality "Gender equality") by having an equal number of events for male and female athletes.[[98]](#cite_note-:0-98) More than 4,400 athletes, including 300 para-athletes, from 71 Commonwealth Games Associations took part in the event.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

The venues such as [Carrara Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Stadium "Carrara Stadium"),[[100]](#cite_note-100) [Carrara Sports and Leisure Centre](/wiki/Carrara_Sports_and_Leisure_Centre "Carrara Sports and Leisure Centre"),[[101]](#cite_note-101) [Carrara Indoor Sports Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Indoor_Stadium "Carrara Indoor Stadium"), [Gold Coast Convention & Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Convention_%26_Exhibition_Centre "Gold Coast Convention & Exhibition Centre").[[102]](#cite_note-102) [Broadbeach Bowls Club](/wiki/Broadbeach_Bowls_Club "Broadbeach Bowls Club")[[103]](#cite_note-103) [Nerang Mountain Bike Trails](/wiki/Nerang_National_Park "Nerang National Park"),[[104]](#cite_note-104) [Coomera Indoor Sports Centre](/wiki/Coomera_Indoor_Sports_Centre "Coomera Indoor Sports Centre"),[[105]](#cite_note-105) [Oxenford Studios](/wiki/Oxenford_Studios "Oxenford Studios"),[[106]](#cite_note-106) The [Gold Coast Hockey Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Hockey_Centre "Gold Coast Hockey Centre"),[[107]](#cite_note-107) [Southport Broadwater Parklands](/wiki/Southport_Broadwater_Parklands "Southport Broadwater Parklands"),[[108]](#cite_note-108) [Gold Coast Aquatic Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Aquatic_Centre "Gold Coast Aquatic Centre"),[[109]](#cite_note-109) [Robina Stadium](/wiki/Robina_Stadium "Robina Stadium"),[[110]](#cite_note-110) The [Currumbin](/wiki/Currumbin,_Queensland "Currumbin, Queensland") Beachfront,[[111]](#cite_note-111) and [Coolangatta](/wiki/Coolangatta "Coolangatta") Beachfront[[112]](#cite_note-112) were used for the Games.

### Olympic and Paralympic Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Olympic and Paralympic Games")]

Gold Coast will be one of the three zones for the [2032 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2032_Summer_Olympics "2032 Summer Olympics") and [2032 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/2032_Summer_Paralympics "2032 Summer Paralympics") in Brisbane to use the venues as the 2018 Commonwealth Games. The Gold Coast Zone will have seven venues, and will host nine Olympic and six Paralympic sports. The [Gold Coast Convention & Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Convention_%26_Exhibition_Centre "Gold Coast Convention & Exhibition Centre") will be used for the preliminary Volleyball along with Powerlifting and Sitting Volleyball during the Paralympics. [Broadbeach Park Stadium](/wiki/Broadbeach,_Queensland "Broadbeach, Queensland") will host Beach Volleyball for Olympics as well in the Football 5-a-side in the Paralympics. The [Gold Coast Sports and Leisure Centre](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Sports_and_Leisure_Centre "Gold Coast Sports and Leisure Centre") will be used for Judo and Wrestling in the Olympics and Boccia in the Paralympics. [Southport Broadwater Parklands](/wiki/Southport_Broadwater_Parklands "Southport Broadwater Parklands") to be used for Triathlon and Marathon Swimming in the Olympics and will be used for Paratriathlon in the Paralympics. [Coomera Indoor Sports Centre](/wiki/Coomera_Indoor_Sports_Centre "Coomera Indoor Sports Centre") will host the Volleyball for the Olympics and will be used for Wheelchair Rugby in the Paralympics. The [Robina Stadium](/wiki/Robina_Stadium "Robina Stadium") will host the preliminary football matches. The [Carrara Stadium](/wiki/Carrara_Stadium "Carrara Stadium") could potentially host the Cricket matches if the IOC approves cricket in the 2028 Olympic Games in Los Angeles.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

### Other events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Other events")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Lexmark_indy_champ_car2_2006.jpg/250px-Lexmark_indy_champ_car2_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lexmark_indy_champ_car2_2006.jpg)

[Gold Coast Indy 300](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Indy_300 "Gold Coast Indy 300")

The [Gold Coast 500](/wiki/Gold_Coast_500 "Gold Coast 500") (formerly known as the Gold Coast 600 & [Gold Coast Indy 300](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Indy_300 "Gold Coast Indy 300")) is a car racing event held annually, usually in October. The [Surfers Paradise Street Circuit](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise_Street_Circuit "Surfers Paradise Street Circuit") through the streets of [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise "Surfers Paradise") and [Main Beach](/wiki/Main_Beach,_Queensland "Main Beach, Queensland"). The Gold Coast 500 typically is accompanied by a range of on and off track events such as the Indy Undie Ball, Miss Indy Competition and the King of Burleigh Hill [Billy cart](/wiki/Billy_cart "Billy cart") Race which take place in the week prior to and/or during the race weekend. Formerly an [IndyCar](/wiki/IndyCar "IndyCar") event, [V8 Supercars](/wiki/Supercars_Championship "Supercars Championship") are now the headline attraction, using a shorter track route, as the circuit was limited following the construction of the [Gold Coast Light Rail](/wiki/G:link "G:link") as the Light Rail line replaced the northbound traffic lanes on Surfers Paradise Boulevard which was previously used as the southern section of the street circuit.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Each June, Coolangatta hosts [Cooly Rocks On](/w/index.php?title=Cooly_Rocks_On&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cooly Rocks On (page does not exist)"), a two-week 1950s and 1960s nostalgia festival with free entertainment and attractions, including [hot rods](/wiki/Hot_rod "Hot rod"), restored cars and revival bands playing music of the era. Every July, more than 25,000 congregate on the Gold Coast from around the world to participate in the [Gold Coast Marathon](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Marathon "Gold Coast Marathon"). It is also the largest annual community sporting event held on the Gold Coast. In 2015, it will be held on 4–5 July and the 37th Gold Coast Airport Marathon is set to motivate and challenge more than 25,000 people of all ages and abilities. The [Gold Coast Airport Marathon](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Airport_Marathon "Gold Coast Airport Marathon") will feature an event for all ages and abilities, including the full Gold Coast Airport Marathon, ASICS Half Marathon, [Southern Cross University](/wiki/Southern_Cross_University "Southern Cross University") 10 kilometres (6.2 mi) Run, Suncorp Bank 5.7 kilometres (3.5 mi) Challenge, and Junior Dash over 4 and 2 kilometres (2.5 and 1.2 mi).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In August [Currumbin](/wiki/Currumbin "Currumbin") hosts the annual half distance Challenge Gold Coast triathlon, with the 1.9 km swim taking place in the Currumbin River, the 90 km bike going through the Currumbin and [Tallebudgera Valleys](/wiki/Tallebudgera_Valley "Tallebudgera Valley") in the Hinterland, and the 21.1 km run going along the beach to Elephant Rock and [Tugun](/wiki/Tugun,_Queensland "Tugun, Queensland").[[114]](#cite_note-114)

Late November to early December sees thousands of school leavers across the country descend on the Gold Coast for [Schoolies week](/wiki/Schoolies_week "Schoolies week"), a two-week period of celebration and parties throughout Surfers Paradise, hosted by the [City of Gold Coast](/wiki/City_of_Gold_Coast "City of Gold Coast"). The event is often criticised nationally and locally for its portrayal of drinking and acts of violence, however every effort by the [Queensland Police Service](/wiki/Queensland_Police_Service "Queensland Police Service") and State Government to ensure all school leavers have a good time are put into place, including locals volunteering by walking the streets and keeping an eye out for those in need of assistance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Early each year the Gold Coast hosts one leg of the [ASP World Tour](/wiki/ASP_World_Tour "ASP World Tour") of surfing, where some of the world's best surfers compete in the [Quiksilver Pro](/wiki/Quiksilver_Pro "Quiksilver Pro") at Coolangatta.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Home of the Arts, Gold Coast](/wiki/Home_of_the_Arts,_Gold_Coast "Home of the Arts, Gold Coast") located in Evandale, features a fine art gallery featuring local and international works from painting to sculpture and new media. In addition, there is a theatre for live productions including musicals as well two arts cinemas showing foreign and independent films from Australia and abroad.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Chinatown, Gold Coast](/wiki/Chinatown,_Gold_Coast "Chinatown, Gold Coast"), located in Southport, hosts the annual citywide Lunar New Year festival as well as regular monthly events.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Media")]

### Print

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Print")]

The daily local newspaper is the *[Gold Coast Bulletin](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Bulletin "Gold Coast Bulletin")* which is published by [News Corporation](/wiki/News_Corporation_(1980%E2%80%932013) "News Corporation (1980–2013)"). National surfing magazine *[Australia's Surfing Life](/wiki/Australia%27s_Surfing_Life "Australia's Surfing Life")* is published in the Gold Coast suburb of [Burleigh Heads](/wiki/Burleigh_Heads "Burleigh Heads") by Morrison Media.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Major daily newspapers such as [The Courier-Mail](/wiki/The_Courier-Mail "The Courier-Mail") and its sibling [The Sunday Mail](/wiki/The_Sunday_Mail_(Brisbane) "The Sunday Mail (Brisbane)") from Brisbane, [The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph_(Sydney) "The Daily Telegraph (Sydney)"), The Sunday Telegraph (Sydney), [The Sydney Morning Herald](/wiki/The_Sydney_Morning_Herald "The Sydney Morning Herald") and [The Sun-Herald](/wiki/The_Sun-Herald "The Sun-Herald") from Sydney and [The Age](/wiki/The_Age "The Age"), [The Sunday Age](/wiki/The_Sunday_Age "The Sunday Age"), [The Herald Sun](/wiki/Herald_Sun "Herald Sun") and the Sunday Herald-Sun from Melbourne as well as national publications [The Australian](/wiki/The_Australian "The Australian") and [The Australian Financial Review](/wiki/The_Australian_Financial_Review "The Australian Financial Review") are all available for purchase on the Gold Coast. Other major interstate newspapers and newspapers from neighbouring regions owned by [News Corporation](/wiki/News_Corporation "News Corporation") or [Australian Community Media](/wiki/Australian_Community_Media "Australian Community Media") are also available for purchase via retail outlets on the Gold Coast.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Television")]

The Gold Coast straddles the boundary between the television licence areas of both Brisbane (metropolitan) and Northern NSW (regional): the Brisbane primary channels are [Seven](/wiki/Seven_Network "Seven Network")'s [BTQ](/wiki/BTQ "BTQ"), [Nine](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network")'s [QTQ](/wiki/QTQ "QTQ") and [10](/wiki/Network_10 "Network 10")'s [TVQ](/wiki/TVQ "TVQ"), while the regional affiliates are [Seven](/wiki/Seven_Network "Seven Network")'s [NEN](/wiki/NEN_(TV_station) "NEN (TV station)"), [Nine](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network")'s [NBN](/wiki/NBN_(TV_station) "NBN (TV station)") and [10](/wiki/Network_10 "Network 10")'s [NRN](/wiki/NRN "NRN").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Both sets of commercial stations are available throughout the Gold Coast, as well as the [ABC](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation") and [SBS](/wiki/Special_Broadcasting_Service "Special Broadcasting Service") television services. Other channels include [10 Drama](/wiki/10_Drama "10 Drama"), [10 Comedy](/wiki/10_Comedy "10 Comedy"), [Nickelodeon](/wiki/Nickelodeon_(Australian_TV_channel) "Nickelodeon (Australian TV channel)"), [Sky News Regional](/wiki/Sky_News_Australia "Sky News Australia") (regional only), [ABC Family](/wiki/ABC_Family_(Australian_TV_channel) "ABC Family (Australian TV channel)")/[ABC Kids](/wiki/ABC_Kids_(Australia) "ABC Kids (Australia)"), [ABC Entertains](/wiki/ABC_Entertains "ABC Entertains"), [ABC News](/wiki/ABC_News_(Australian_TV_channel) "ABC News (Australian TV channel)"), [SBS World Movies](/wiki/SBS_World_Movies "SBS World Movies"), [SBS Viceland](/wiki/SBS_Viceland "SBS Viceland"), [SBS Food](/wiki/SBS_Food "SBS Food"), [NITV](/wiki/National_Indigenous_Television "National Indigenous Television"), [SBS WorldWatch](/wiki/SBS_WorldWatch "SBS WorldWatch"), [7two](/wiki/7two "7two"), [7mate](/wiki/7mate "7mate"), [7Bravo](/wiki/7Bravo "7Bravo"), [7flix](/wiki/7flix "7flix"), [9Gem](/wiki/9Gem "9Gem"), [9Go!](/wiki/9Go! "9Go!"), [9Rush](/wiki/9Rush "9Rush") & [9Life](/wiki/9Life "9Life"). Subscription television service [Foxtel](/wiki/Foxtel "Foxtel") is also available.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Of the main metropolitan and regional commercial networks:

* [Nine News](/wiki/Nine_Gold_Coast_News "Nine Gold Coast News") produces a half-hour local bulletin at 5:30pm on weeknights, broadcasting from studios in [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise "Surfers Paradise"). The bulletin airs as an opt-out[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] on the metropolitan station ([QTQ](/wiki/QTQ "QTQ")9) ahead of the main 6 pm news from Brisbane.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Seven News](/wiki/Seven_News#Gold_Coast "Seven News") produced a local half-hour bulletin at 5:30pm on weeknights from studios in Surfers Paradise until 21 November 2024. The network retains a newsroom to cover the Gold Coast for its state and national bulletins.[[116]](#cite_note-116)
* [NBN](/wiki/NBN_(TV_station) "NBN (TV station)") airs *NBN News*, an hour-long regional program combining regional, national and international news – including local opt-outs for the Gold Coast and Northern Rivers – every night at 6pm. It broadcasts from studios in [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales") with reporters based in Lismore and Surfers Paradise.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Network 10](/wiki/Network_10 "Network 10") airs short local news updates for the Gold Coast/Lismore district throughout the day from the [WIN Television](/wiki/WIN_Television "WIN Television") studios in Wollongong.

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Radio")]

There are numerous commercial, ABC, narrowcast and community stations broadcasting along the Gold Coast.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Gold Coast's [FM](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") commercial stations are [Triple M Network](/wiki/Triple_M "Triple M")'s [92.5 Triple M](/wiki/Triple_M_Gold_Coast "Triple M Gold Coast"), [Hit Network](/wiki/Hit_Network "Hit Network")'s [90.9 Sea FM](/wiki/90.9_Sea_FM "90.9 Sea FM"), [Hot Tomato](/wiki/Hot_Tomato "Hot Tomato"), [Rebel 99.4 FM](/wiki/Rebel_FM "Rebel FM"), [The Breeze 100.6](/wiki/The_Breeze_(Australia) "The Breeze (Australia)") and [Radio 97](/wiki/Radio_97 "Radio 97"). Community/volunteer stations are [94.1FM](/wiki/Jazz_Radio_94.1FM "Jazz Radio 94.1FM"), [Juice107.3](/wiki/Juice107.3 "Juice107.3"), [Radio Metro](/wiki/Radio_Metro "Radio Metro") and 4CRB.

[ABC Gold Coast](/wiki/ABC_Gold_Coast "ABC Gold Coast") is the local ABC station on the Gold Coast, which is complemented by the ABC's national radio services including [Triple J](/wiki/Triple_J "Triple J"), [ABC Radio National](/wiki/ABC_Radio_National "ABC Radio National"), [ABC NewsRadio](/wiki/ABC_NewsRadio "ABC NewsRadio") and [ABC Classic](/wiki/ABC_Classic "ABC Classic").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A number of narrowcast services are also available on the Gold Coast including [Big Country Radio](/w/index.php?title=Big_Country_Radio&action=edit&redlink=1 "Big Country Radio (page does not exist)"), [SEN Track 1620](/wiki/SEN_Track "SEN Track"), [Raw FM](/wiki/Raw_FM_(Australian_radio_network) "Raw FM (Australian radio network)") and [Vision Radio](/wiki/Vision_Radio_Network "Vision Radio Network").

The Gold Coast can also receive some Brisbane and Northern NSW FM and AM stations.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/GBC-ARI-DJI_0662.jpg/250px-GBC-ARI-DJI_0662.jpg)](/wiki/File:GBC-ARI-DJI_0662.jpg)

[Bond University](/wiki/Bond_University "Bond University") in [Varsity Lakes](/wiki/Varsity_Lakes,_Queensland "Varsity Lakes, Queensland")

### Colleges and universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Colleges and universities")]

The Gold Coast is home to two major university campuses: [Bond University](/wiki/Bond_University "Bond University") at [Robina](/wiki/Robina,_Queensland "Robina, Queensland") and [Griffith University](/wiki/Griffith_University "Griffith University") at [Southport](/wiki/Southport,_Queensland "Southport, Queensland"). [Southern Cross University](/wiki/Southern_Cross_University "Southern Cross University") also operates a smaller campus in [Bilinga](/wiki/Bilinga,_Queensland "Bilinga, Queensland") near the Gold Coast Airport. [TAFE Queensland](/wiki/TAFE_Queensland "TAFE Queensland") also has five campuses at Southport, Robina, Benowa, Coomera and Coolangatta.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Schools and libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Coast,_Queensland&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Schools and libraries")]

There are over 100 primary and secondary schools, both public and private and of a variety of denominations, including the selective state high school [Queensland Academy for Health Sciences](/wiki/Queensland_Academy_for_Health_Sciences "Queensland Academy for Health Sciences") and [single-sex](/wiki/Single-sex_education "Single-sex education") private schools [The Southport School](/wiki/The_Southport_School "The Southport School") and [St Hilda's School](/wiki/St_Hilda%27s_School "St Hilda's School"). The longest established public school on the Gold Coast is [Southport State High School](/wiki/Southport_State_High_School "Southport State High School"), having originally opened in 1916. There are a number of libraries located on the Gold Coast. For a full list see [Gold Coast libraries](/wiki/Gold_Coast_libraries "Gold Coast libraries").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
